data.title: Oddity Tech: When Beauty Becomes A Data Business
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Oddity (ODD) offers a rare combination of high growth, profitability, and a competitive advantage built on data within the beauty industry, offering differentiation from other traditional beauty brands.

After a 50% pullback since mid-2025, the market is pricing the company at a compelling valuation again, creating an attractive entry point if Oddity continues expanding its brand portfolio and demonstrates that its current success is replicable.

Innovation in the Beauty Industry

Oddity is an Israeli technology company specializing in the beauty industry. Its value proposition isn't that of a typical cosmetics company that simply sells a predefined product. Instead, it uses machine learning and hyperspectral imaging to create products tailored to individual needs, analyzing a user's skin tone, undertones, texture, wrinkles, pores, and even moisture levels rather than formulating a product and hoping it fits the greatest number of people.

The company achieves this by feeding millions of data points into algorithms that predict what will work best for each specific customer, a fundamentally different approach from traditional beauty companies.

It currently operates a portfolio of brands that includes IL MAKIAGE (high-performance makeup) and SpoiledChild (skincare and supplements), both driven by direct-to-consumer models, and has allowed Oddity to reach $657 million in revenue in the first 9 months of 2025, or a year-over-year growth of 25%, with a high gross margin of 73% and already being profitable both operationally and in net income.

In addition to these two brands, Oddity is actively expanding its portfolio through new, internally developed concepts. Brand 3, now officially introduced as METHODIQ, marks the company's entry into clinical-grade skincare powered by molecular discovery and is designed to offer formulations tailored to individual biological profiles and represents Oddity's most technologically ambitious attempt to date at building a personalized skincare ecosystem. Brand 4, still unrevealed publicly. And beyond its owned brands, Oddity is also building a space for Partner Brands, which allows third-party companies to leverage Oddity's technology and data, which also broadens Oddity's ecosystem without requiring full ownership of every product line.

The Competitive Advantage of Data

As you can already guess, one of the most important aspects of Oddity's business model and the real reason it can scale faster than traditional beauty companies is its data moat. Unlike most cosmetics brands, which rely on a mix of retail feedback, trend forecasting, and limited consumer testing, Oddity operates a large data infrastructure that collects inputs from every customer interaction. Each time a user uploads a photo or answers a diagnostic quiz, that information is fed back into the same machine learning and creates a circle in which the more customers the company serves, the more data it collects, and the more data it collects, the more accurate its algorithms become at predicting which formulation will perform best for each individual consumer. A feedback system that traditional beauty companies cannot replicate easily because they don't own direct consumer relationships at scale since retailers like Sephora, Ulta, or department stores sit in the middle and control this important data.

This data then enables Oddity to launch new brands with lower risk and faster iteration cycles than traditional players. And as more partner brands join the ecosystem, the company could collect even more consumer insights without taking on the operational burden of owning every product line. This is interesting because it could transform the company into more of a technology company than a consumer one in the future, once it has accumulated a large volume of data and can even charge other brands for this valuable information.

Valuation

This dynamic helps explain why Oddity has been consistently raising its guidance throughout the year. As the company scales, the predictive accuracy of its models improves, customer acquisition becomes more efficient, and all of this has translated into accelerating growth.

Considering the most recent guidance and growth plans through new brands, I think it's reasonable to estimate that the company will grow its sales by almost 20% annually on average for the next few years. Furthermore, improvements in operational efficiency thanks to a direct-to-consumer model without intermediaries and scalable technology should drive margin expansion. This means that within five years the company could be generating more than $250 million in profit. If we divide that among the 61 million shares outstanding, assuming they will no longer dilute because they are already profitable, the estimated EPS would be between $4 and $4.5.

Now comes the tricky part, since the 20x earnings multiple is reasonable according to the industry average, but if Oddity ends up becoming more of a high-margin data provider than a makeup product company, this multiple could be even higher. But in my base scenario, I'll simply multiply the EPS by 20, and the result would be a potential share price of $85 or a rather appealing 18% CAGR.

Risks

While Oddity's business is differentiated and its growth prospects are strong, there are risks that one should keep in mind, like the company's heavy reliance on its data to sustain product accuracy and customer acquisition efficiency. Oddity's predictive engine improves as it gathers more data, but this also means that any disruption, such as privacy regulation changes, limits on biometric data usage, or shifts in consumer behavior around data sharing, could materially affect the core of its competitive advantage. Traditional beauty companies may not have comparable datasets, but they do have massive budgets and distribution power, meaning that if large competitors accelerate their investments in AI-driven personalization, the gap could narrow.

Another significant risk is tied to Oddity's brand concentration since currently its sales are made up of only two brands. For odds to justify long-term high growth, new brands like METHODIQ or Brand 4 must demonstrate that they are a viable concept and scale successfully. This adds execution risk if any of these new ventures fail to have the expected success.

The Bottom Line

Oddity is not just another beauty brand; it's building a database that truly differentiates it from traditional players in the industry. With IL MAKIAGE and SpoiledChild already scaling at a profitable pace and new initiatives like METHODIQ adding growth in the coming years, the company appears to have a bright future ahead, and its high growth demonstrates traction.

That said, this is still an early-stage company operating in a competitive sector, so the next few quarters will test Oddity's ability to expand beyond its first two brands and prove that its technology platform can repeatedly produce successful new categories. After dropping 50% since June 2025, I think the risk-reward profile has improved considerably, and I could now consider it a buy.

data.title: Independence Realty Trust: The Bullish Case For A REIT Betting On Sun Belt Growth
data.description:
Today's Pick: A Residential REIT in the S&P 400 MidCap

Although I often write about REITs and even launched a book on that topic this month, there is one multifamily residential REIT I have not covered yet but definitely deserves it, and that is Independence Realty Trust (IRT), whose Seeking Alpha profile describes it as an "S&P 400 MidCap Company" and one that "owns and operates multifamily communities across non-gateway U.S. markets."

Notably, as a company, it has a $4.1B market cap and an average trading volume of over 2MM shares, so it is certainly no small fry in the pond, and currently its dividend yield is topping +4%, so it is on my dividend watchlist as well.

My Rating: Buy

For my very first coverage of IRT, I am calling it a buy, and the upside factors helping drive this score include favorable macro signals in the residential REIT sector, particularly the Sun Belt region; this REIT's favorable occupancy rate and portfolio growth; and a very attractive balance sheet profile with a BBB rating from Fitch, as well as a nice dividend recovery trend post-pandemic.

The worksheet below visualizes how I came to my rating score.

My rating methodology separately scores each of 8 categories in this table, and a more detailed discussion follows below.

Long-Term Return vs. S&P 500: Despite Underperforming Index, It Beat Key REIT Peers

Let's start off by setting the stage for this stock and showing what kind of momentum it has had historically against a benchmark like the S&P 500 vs. 4 similar peers to get an idea of proven ability to outperform the benchmark over time, assuming, for example, that an alternative is to invest in an S&P 500 index fund.

My peer comparables are other multi-family residential REITs in the US: Veris (VRE), BRT Apartments (BRT), Equity Residential (EQR), and UDR (UDR), with an effort made to pick REITs across a diverse geography of the US.

In this example I made using momentum data, we can see that although IRT is trailing the benchmark over a 10-year period, there were instances where it did outperform it (such as where I circled in 2021), showing that it could be prone to momentum spikes.

With a 10-year price return of +116%, IRT actually had the best return of this peer group, with some peers even showing negative returns (such as VRE with -37%). What this could mean from a future-looking view is that this REIT could statistically be more likely to have a positive price return than some similar peers, but of course that is a projection at this point, which is why this topic is only one of many in this rating.

Because of outperformance vs. this peer group but underperforming the benchmark, I am inclined to give this section a low buy score nevertheless.

Sector Risk: Continued Supply/Demand Imbalance Could Favor Sun Belt REITs

Moving on, we should consider sector risk specific to multi-family residential REITs, as opposed to all REITs, since this niche is not quite the same as industrial, retail, or farmland REITs, for instance.

We can see from the Nov 2025 investor deck, for instance, that this REIT is both diversified geographically and also has at least 73% of its NOI tied to the US Sun Belt, with 3 of its top 5 markets considered Sun Belt areas (Dallas, Tampa, and Atlanta):

This REIT, therefore, could see continued upside if there continues to be population growth in the Sun Belt regions, particularly in their existing markets, as long as there is also not considerable new supply getting built to meet the demand, since oversupply of apartments could dampen rents.

My personal experience in the so-called Sun Belt is having lived/worked in Austin, Texas (where, incidentally, my equities research firm, Albert Anthony & Co., is also registered as a business), and I understand the appeal of more space and amenities for lower rents than in some northeast cities, not to mention milder winters.

But is this an ongoing trend or a fad that could fade soon? Let's see what some data says.

Back in 2023 data, for instance, official forecasts from the City of Dallas Economic Development agency showed that "if current trends continue, the metroplex is on target to surpass Chicago to become the 3rd largest metropolitan area in the U.S. in 2030:"

Besides Dallas, more recent data and forecasts continue pointing to Tampa, Florida, as another growth engine, with Tampa Bay Business & Wealth Magazine writing this last January:

Tampa Bay's technology sector is on a robust growth trajectory, with significant developments anticipated in 2025. The region has experienced a 30% increase in IT job growth, over the past five years, and is projected to add over 3,700 jobs by 2027, marking a 14% growth, according to the City of Tampa.

In terms of another market this REIT is in, Atlanta, some evidence points to a supply/demand imbalance. JPMorgan (JPM) wrote in a housing topic article this month that "the region's population has surged, but housing production has not kept up with demand," further adding that "new buyers face costs rising from 25% of income in 2019 to 40% today—potentially putting homeownership out of reach for many."

Well, to echo what my late great-uncle used to say, after having built a successful modular construction business in the Pacific Northwest in the 1980s, "Everyone needs a home," and that saying of his made me think that the more difficult it is to buy a home today for many, the more people will depend on renting, and that is not so bad for a REIT like this that owns apartments in growth regions of the US.

So, I am continuing to be bullish on the Sun Belt and therefore am giving this category a strong buy score.

Company Growth: New Acquisitions, High Occupancy, and New Inventory Low

Now, we have some idea of the macro elements at play in the residential REIT market, but there are also company-specific growth factors to consider going forward, and for that I turned to the most recent company earnings releases and investor decks.

Although I've noticed REITs often sell or dispose of certain properties for various reasons, sometimes perhaps to raise cash or reinvest in properties with higher returns, portfolio growth is something I look for since new units coming online can drive future rental revenues.

In that regard, Q3 results commentary, under investment activity, mentioned the firm acquired a 240-unit property in Orlando, Florida, and a 403-unit property in Orlando, saying, "The acquisitions increased our exposure in Orlando from 617 units to 1,260 units."

Stable and high occupancy is also critical for the top line, so we can also see from the November investor deck (pg. 4) that over the year shown, this firm has managed to keep occupancy around 95%, which I think is pretty high (above 90%):

As far as challenges from new supply filling its key markets, the company's own data presented in its investor deck (pg. 11) shows that due to the time it takes to actually construct and deliver a ready-to-move-in apartment complex and the decline in new construction starts, there could continue to be muted inventory:

I believe this low-inventory scenario, though perhaps not the best for renters looking for amazing deals on rent and lots of choices, is favorable to the upside for a REIT like this.

The recipe above makes me inclined to give this category a strong buy score.

Profit Margins: Respectable EBITDA Margin, AFFO Growth Metrics, and Better ROI via Upgrades

While macro trends and portfolio growth vs. low supply sound like a winning combo, investors should also consider where this REIT stands with its profit margins vs. some other similar REITs to get a sense of how efficient it may be in generating future profits.

Because a REIT, and many I've written about here, are known to use non-cash depreciation expense to legally lower reported net income, sometimes net income margins by themselves may give a skewed picture.

So, let's focus more on the EBITDA margin (which excludes depreciation), and Seeking Alpha data shows this REIT is highly competitive in this metric among these peers shown:

With an EBITDA margin of +56%, IRT finds itself in the top 3 of this peer group. Now, if you place importance on net margin too, you can see it has a positive net margin of +3.3% as well and has been profitable in 6 of the last 7 quarters, as shown in the income statement.

For a REIT, however, adjusted FFO growth should also be considered, and for this REIT we see from growth data that its expected FWD AFFO growth is positive at +1.1%, but also that its 5-year AFFO growth CAGR is at +9.4%, while the sector average was just 2.6%. The AFFO metric is important as it can impact the ability to cover dividend payouts too.

I should also point out a notable fact about this REIT that can impact expenses, and that is an ongoing strategy to upgrade certain units to drive value-add. While this is an expense, it seems the goal is to generate future efficiencies and higher rents from those units.

For instance, the recent investor deck (pg. 18) highlighted one example in Columbus, Ohio, where "upgrades drove a 48% average rent increase and delivered a 28% unlevered ROI."

Incidentally, Columbus, Ohio, is also tied to family history, as a distant cousin on my maternal side first immigrated to America over a century ago and ended up in Columbus when it was largely a blue-collar industrial center home to steel and iron factories, which I mention because it would have been unheard of in his era of the 1950s to pay $1,400 for renting there because often people were happy to have a roof over their head; however, the case example above shows that not only do times change but also that REITs actively upgrading properties are able to justify higher rents by presenting them to tenants willing and able to spend more for newer amenities, driving ROI for the REIT.

The combination of factors mentioned in this section supports a bullish justification in this category.

Dividend Growth and Coverage: Modest Payout Ratio and Post-Pandemic Recovery Trend

Here, let's consider some dividend metrics, since I think a major appeal of REIT investing is the dividend income, though I realize it is not every investor's main goal since dividends can be taxable.

If yield is your main focus, with a yield of around 4%, IRT trails several peers where you can get a better return in this metric, including BRT with a nearly 7% yield, as peer data shows:

However, since yield can often fluctuate as share price moves or dividend payouts change, another metric I track is long-term dividend growth.

I was curious why this REIT shows a 10-year dividend CAGR of -0.87%, and then a closer look reveals that this REIT seems to have been impacted by the pandemic years (2020/2021), when it lowered its dividend; however, it seems to be back on a recovery track since then, and that is a positive upside signal.

I am also looking to see if the payout ratio between dividends and AFFO is under 75%, leaving plenty of cash cushion.

We can see from income statement data, for instance, that in the quarter ending September, the AFFO (diluted) per share was $0.30, and if the quarterly dividend is $0.17, that is around a 56% payout ratio, which is well below my benchmark of 75% and leaves quite a cushion.

So, while it may be less attractive than some REITs in terms of dividend yield, the bullish case can be supported by the post-pandemic dividend recovery trend but also the AFFO coverage and modest payout ratio.

Balance Sheet Risk: A Very Low D/E, Strong Fitch Rating, and Asset Geographic Diversification

When it comes to balance sheet risk, although we won't go over every single risk that could exist, I narrowed it down to the D/E ratio, asset exposure, and credit ratings.

On a positive note, IRT has a debt-to-equity ratio of just 0.66, and that greatly boosts its upside picture as it shows low leverage risk in relation to equity and similar peers. For instance, another residential REIT with a nationwide portfolio, BRT, has a much higher D/E at 2.66.

Second, notably, last February a major agency like Fitch affirmed an investment-grade BBB rating for this REIT, saying in their rating note that "the ratings reflect IRT's solid balance sheet, stable property performance, and execution of its portfolio optimization plan and deleveraging strategy," while also cautioning that a downgrade could occur in the event of "sustained negative operating fundamentals."

Such a positive third-party perspective certainly adds color to the overall picture for this REIT.

I have been keeping an eye on upgrades or increased investment into this REIT by major Wall St. firms, and notable is this week's news in a financial portal that Nomura (NMR) has been adding, more specifically, "Nomura Asset Management Co. grew its stake in Independence Realty Trust, Inc. by 4.7% during the second quarter."

I believe that could indicate firms like Nomura view the risk/benefit scenario of this REIT to be positive, and when we speak of risk, we cannot ignore asset risk exposure since, in this case, IRT is exposed to considerable non-liquid assets like real estate. In fact, balance sheet data shows "Total Real Estate Assets" of $5.7B as of the quarter ending September.

Asset risk could come from a major weather/natural/fire catastrophe impacting a metro area or entire region, a major demographic shift to the downside leading to falling property values, or high tenant delinquency rates impacting rent income.

What I think mitigates this risk is shown in the investor deck (pg. 7), which is that its top 5 markets are spread across different metro areas of the US, and together they make up less than 50% of NOI:

So, given the multiple factors above, this section also leads to a bullish sentiment, as in previous ones.

Chart Technical Indicators: Awaiting the Next Clear Buying Signal

In this section, to change up somewhat from only talking about fundamental analysis, I've included some technicals too.

This chart shows the relationship between the 50-day simple moving average and the 200-day over a 1-year period:

Using a common technical indicator, the death cross and the golden cross, we see that a death cross (red circle) happened last spring when the 50-day SMA crossed below the 200-day SMA, and there has not yet been a reversal of this to form a golden cross again, which would be a buy signal usually.

For this reason, I am inclined to be neutral/hold in this category, and that lowers the overall rating somewhat. Now, since it has been so long since the last death cross, some readers may challenge this view and say a reversal is ripe for happening soon, and so I welcome your comments on the above chart. I am leaning towards a hold in this case as the 50-day and 200-day are running parallel essentially.

Valuation vs. Future Upside: Some Undervaluation Signals, Along With Future Upside Forecast

Coming to the last discussion topic, we will briefly discuss this stock's valuation vs. peers and also future price forecasts.

Using Seeking Alpha valuation data, we see that IRT is somewhat undervalued compared to this peer group average, both in forward price to FFO and also price to book:

Its book value per share is only $14.60, so it is still trading at a premium to book (share price of $16.94) compared to some hotel REITs I covered lately trading below book value. I think this is because the market sees future growth in its portfolio value driven by Sun Belt growth, and the hotel REIT sector can be more recessionary than a residential portfolio.

The slight undervaluation to peers on FWD P/FFO is not significant, nor does it make this REIT a major outlier, but as I showed earlier, its EBITDA margin is beaten by at least two of these peers, and I think serious investors have noticed by now.

Whether it is an undervaluation opportunity also depends on future price upside, so my worksheet below sheds some light there:

From the table above, my price target is based on the FFO consensus for Dec 2026 (since we are almost done with 2025), and it implies a +4% upside, whereas the average Wall St consensus target implies a +22% upside.

When both targets, mine and Wall St., imply a positive upside well above 1-2%, I think it adds to the buy argument, and in this case it is a slightly undervalued stock with future upside expected.

The bullish sentiment in this section adds to the overall buy case for this stock and reinforces it nicely.

Challenge to My Rating: Interest Rate Risk

A challenge to my bullish rating that has not been included in my rating or evaluation could come from interest rate risk.

This sector loves low cost of capital and low-interest-rate environments in which to invest and grow its real estate portfolio. Investors new to REITs particularly should be aware that forces outside the control of this REIT can impact the cost of capital, since this REIT does not decide what interest rate it will pay on commercial loans, but rather that can be impacted by outside factors including Fed decisions and US Treasury yields.

In fact, I made a table of 4 key factors impacting the "future" cost of capital, since I am primarily focused on the future and not past or historical risk:

At least one of those factors, Fed decisions, can somewhat be gauged from CME FedWatch probability forecasts, and as of this writing, there is a 69% probability of another rate cut this December, which is up from the last time I checked, so that could be a mitigating factor to interest rate risk.

Conclusion: Agree With Buy Consensus From Wall St. and Analysts

To conclude today, my buy rating agrees with the consensus from Wall St. and Seeking Alpha analysts but is slightly more bullish than the quant rating of hold.

My call to action for investors would be that this is not only a dividend idea but also an undervaluation opportunity to capture future upside and own a piece of what I think is one of the lower-risk REIT sectors, multi-family residential, and the fundamentals of this company have shown that it can not only compete with several peers but even outperform them in some areas.

data.title: Palantir's Ontology: A Buzzword For An Ordinary Idea
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Last week, Seeking Alpha's Julian Lin made a strong argument against Palantir (PLTR) - a high price multiple that will take years for the company to grow into. I agree. The stock is priced for perfection that almost never happens in real life. There are two conditions that, if met, would give some sense to PLTR's valuation. First, if its products are indispensable. Second, if it has a very wide tech moat, it is hard to replicate its offerings, shielding it from competition.

PLTR's US commercial sales grew by a whopping 121% in the third quarter, but I believe that management's bold claims about the disruptive and indispensable nature of their software are bombastic.

We are the only platform bringing true transformational impact to the enterprise AI market - PLTR CRO, Q3'25

Organizations are embracing an undeniable truth. Real enterprise AI at scale requires Palantir. We're seeing that AIP again and again, is the only platform delivering transformational impact in this market - PLTR CRO, Q3'25

Realizing value from AI in the enterprise requires the Elian integration of LLMs, workflow and software, and that's only possible with ontology. PLTR CTO, Q2'25

LLMs simply don't work in the real world without Palantir - PLTR's CRO, Q2'25

We see an increased recognition that large language models were commodities, that the value of these commodities becomes only realizable in the context of our ontology on top of Foundry - PLTR's CEO, Q1'25

There's a consensus in the market that Palantir owns value creation - PLTR's CEO, Q1'25

PLTR is one of the few companies that caters to retail investors, reading questions submitted online, and even allowing some retail shareholders to participate in the earnings calls. The ticker is often described as a meme stock.

But here's the dilemma facing current investors. PLTR's stock price might fluctuate with market sentiment, as it did this month (shares are down 25% in November), but valuation won't fundamentally reset until growth slows enough to convince retail investors that a lot of PLTR's management remarks might not have been intended to be taken at face value. Until then, the bullish trade will continue, on the back of the strong growth momentum, as highlighted by fellow analyst Yiannis Zourmpanos.

Beyond PLTR's relationship with the government, there are big questions over its claims regarding Foundry and AIP.

What Does Palantir Do?

The obscurity of PLTR's software has arguably given it a false aura of depth and uniqueness. The fact is, "ontology" isn't really deep. At its core, it's assigning labels to objects and drawing relations between said labels with a write-back feature. A product is assigned a "product_name", "product_weight", "supplier_id", "warehouse_location", "order_number", etc, before relationships are drawn between these objects.

This is not to say that the engineering is trivial. It's not. PLTR is known for its military-grade quality. The things that a company can do when its databases are interconnected by a common language aren't something to scoff at.

But generative AI has raised the bar of what is considered innovative software, and while PLTR's product is of high quality, it doesn't meet that bar. A simple relational database inherently utilises ontology. Almost every enterprise software uses ontology.

A recent peer-reviewed paper in Nature magazine defines ontology as:

Ontologies are well-structured vocabularies that logically define classes and relationships in the interest of promoting interoperability.

Rishi Jaluria, Royal Bank of Canada analyst, said in an interview with Yahoo Finance, as quoted by The Register:

I just don't think Palantir is that cutting-edge generative AI company that they claim it to be. It is a good data pipeline. It is a good data ontology company. I'm not saying it's not a useful company, but it is not what they claim it is.

Why are US Commercial Sales Rising?

Gotham, PLTR's government software, was its first product. Foundry, a commercial twist of Gotham, was released in 2016, but hasn't achieved success until recently. It wasn't a long time ago that PLTR injected cash into obscure SPACs, under what it called "Strategic Investment Program", in return for commitments to buy PLTR's software. One year ago, PLTR's US commercial sales stood at $179 million, which isn't particularly impressive in the enterprise software market, where 8-figure contracts are the standard.

But the segment's sales grew by 121% in Q3'25, and management says this is because their software is indispensable in the generative AI era, and that no company can do what they do. These claims are critical to justify the company's valuation.

But, how true are these claims?

First, I think that US Commercial sales growth isn't proof of these claims. The segment's 121% growth in Q3'25 benefited from a small sales base in the same quarter of last year: $179 million to be exact. From such a low revenue base, a handful of standard contracts could easily create such high growth rates. The company added $218 million to US commercial sales in the twelve months ended September 2025. Intuit (INTU), the tax software firm, added $1.1 billion in the same period, ~5 times more than PLTR. Salesforce and ServiceNow increased their sales (mostly subscription revenue), by $700 million and $610 million, respectively, demonstrating better performance and market acceptance, casting doubt over PLTR's claims.

Secondly, the rise of generative AI has accelerated digitalisation trends, leading to a rush in demand for digital solutions, including those provided by PLTR. It's a wave that lifts all boats. The mediocre sales before the generative AI bonanza cast doubt over PLTR's Foundry offering. As mentioned in the previous article, PLTR's Foundry is customised for each customer, and while this lends flexibility, it also means higher costs, including a recurring cost of ownership.

Having said that, there is truth to the claim that PLTR's software could help companies build their generative AI functions. But saying that PLTR is the only software that does that is false. Microsoft, Snowflake (SNOW), Alphabet (GOOGL), and Amazon (AMZN), Databricks, all offer tech stacks that connect enterprise data with LLMs with privacy and control dashboards, including Synapse, Fabric, Sagemaker, Vertex AI, Mosaic AI, and many others.

For example, PLTR states that its AIP can be used for Retrieval Augmented Generation "RAG", train and deploy LLMs, governance, and testing and evaluation. But these are the same functions and capabilities of other offerings, such as Mosaic AI and Snowflake, as noted in a recent article by CloudKaptan.

Mosaic AI by Databricks is a unified platform purpose-built for building, deploying, and governing production-grade Generative AI systems. Built on top of the Databricks Lakehouse, it seamlessly integrates the full AI lifecycle from data preparation and prompt engineering to LLM fine-tuning, deployment, and monitoring with robust enterprise governance - CloudKaptan, July 2025

Valuation

Salesforce's revenue is approximately ten times PLTR's ($39.5 billion vs $3.9 billion TTM, yet PLTR's market cap is about 71% more than Salesforce, despite PLTR's share price decline last week.

At a forward Price/Sales ratio of 94x, and a forward P/E ratio of 240x, PLTR will need at least a decade of uninterrupted 30% growth to reach the valuation multiples of big tech such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta (META), on a P/S basis, assuming its share price remains at today's levels for the next decade. With dilution, the dynamics are even worse.

Below is the Price/Sales ratio of PLTR compared to large tech.

This shows that the company is priced for perfection. Any stumble or crack in the growth narrative in this period would result in a price multiple correction, leading to significant capital losses.

Final Thoughts and Risks

PLTR's Foundry and the AIP are powerful solutions to a very specific problem: complex, real-time, security-sensitive decision-making. This is a niche within the enterprise software market, and arguably a small one. If this hypothesis is true, then PLTR's growth will slow fast, and once it does, those +200x multiples are unlikely to be seen again.

I don't accept the premise that PLTR's software is critical to generative AI deployment, a claim repeated by management. I think calling it an operating system is confusing, to say the least. Ontology isn't an engineering breakthrough.

The main risk to this Sell rating is the short-term sales momentum. As long as PLTR is delivering triple-digit growth, the valuation multiples will fluctuate at current levels, rebounding with the broader indexes even after macro-related selloffs.

data.title: Pipeline Momentum Drives C4 Therapeutics Despite Q3 EPS Miss
data.description:
Thesis

C4 Therapeutics (CCCC) reported a GAAP EPS loss of -$0.44 for 3Q25. A figure that missed analyst estimates by about $0.03. Revenue, on the other hand, came in at $11.23 million, beating expectations by $4.95 million. It's a bit of nice progress on collaboration programs for investors, despite lower milestone contributions compared to the prior year. In terms of cash, the company has about $199.8 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, but that's before factoring in a number of equity raises that have taken place over the past few months. The company just recently filed a hefty $400 million mixed shelf offering. It should allow the company to raise capital through things like common stock, preferred stock, debt, warrants, or units in one or more tranches going forward. The filing should give them a bit of flexibility to support the upcoming clinical pipeline trials, which I'll discuss. Shares can be picked up in the mid-$2 range, and I think they still have a lot of room to run with some key upcoming catalysts coming early next year.

Earnings

As you know, C4 has been investing heavily in the development of its lead degrader programs over the past few quarters. It's been a fine balance between heavy investing and trying to find some long-term financial stability. Revenue for the quarter came in at just $11.2 million, down slightly from the $15.4 million we saw in 3Q24. This was primarily due to a prior-year milestone payment from Biogen; however, this was partially offset by recognition of deferred revenue from the Merck collaboration. Operating expenses totalled a hefty $45.6 million, slightly higher than the prior year because of a $10.7 million impairment charge, though both R&D and G&A costs actually declined meaningfully since the expensive clinical activities for CFT1946 are winding down. Stock-based compensation also decreased.

Overall, the company reported a net loss of $32.2 million, a bit more than the $24.7 million loss they posted in 3Q24. The loss was mostly driven by the impairment charge and lower collaboration revenue. C4 managed to end the quarter with $199.8 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, which, keep in mind, doesn't include the $125 million equity raise completed in October. Management has tipped this cash position to give an operational runway through the end of 2028. This should be well past some key clinical inflexion points, which we will discuss soon, including the upcoming registrational Phase 2 MOMENTUM trial of cemsidomide. The news of earnings didn't result in too much stock movement; shares are up just 2.5% since the announcement on November 6th.

Pipeline potential

Now, C4's pipeline is, of course, led by cemsidomide. It's an IKZF1/3 degrader that has already completed Phase 1 in multiple myeloma and is now moving into a registrational Phase 2 trial sometime in 1Q26. There is also a Phase 1b combination study with elranatamab set to start in 2Q26. Elsewhere, CFT8919, an allosteric Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor L858R degrader for non-small cell lung cancer, is currently in an ongoing Phase 1 dose-escalation trial in Greater China through the company's partner Betta Pharmaceuticals. CFT1946 is another treatment in the pipeline, tipped to be a BRAF V600 degrader. It has already completed Phase 1; however, the program is currently paused since C4 is actively seeking partnership options for that pipeline section.

​​Cemsidomide

So, ​​Cemsidomide would be their lead investigational IKZF1/3 degrader. It's currently being developed for relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma. Mechanistically, cemsidomide works by using the body's natural protein degradation system. It would then selectively degrade the transcription factors IKZF1 and IKZF3, which are the main drivers of multiple myeloma cell survival. It has just completed its Phase 1 trial, which also includes full enrollment and dose escalation. From the trial, the data showed a potential best-in-class profile, with overall response rates of roughly 40% at 75 µg and 53% at 100 µg. Now, this also includes a minimal residual disease-negative complete response at the highest dose they administered. Responses were also pretty durable, with a median duration of response of 9.3 months. The other good news was that the treatment was well-tolerated with a differentiated safety profile compared to other IKZF1/3 degraders. So, based on these results, cemsidomide is now advancing into two major next steps: a registrational Phase 2 MOMENTUM trial in combination with dexamethasone, and a Phase 1b trial combining it with Pfizer's elranatamab.

These two trials should serve as the next big catalysts for C4 stock. Year to date, the stock finds itself down about 33%, but we have to factor in the momentum killer that was Liberation Day. Post April 22nd, the stock is actually up about 56% and has shown pretty strong momentum. The bullish take would be for this stock's momentum to continue into 2026, leading up to the trial readout.

Cemsidomide actually targets quite a large multiple myeloma market. C4 estimates the addressable population across multiple myeloma to be roughly 65,000 patients in 1L, 56,000 in 2L, 49,000 in 3L, 42,000 in 4L, and just over 23,000 in 5L+ in the U.S., EU4, and UK. The company's model projects potential peak annual revenues of about $1 billion for the 5L+ dexamethasone combo, which may grow to over $6 billion if cemsidomide is used in combination with a BCMA bispecific or anti-CD38 agent. On the market right now, its main competitors would be lenalidomide (Revlimid) and pomalidomide (Pomalyst). Both are well-established IKZF1/3-targeting therapies, which sell for roughly $26,000 per month in the U.S. for Revlimid, with Pomalyst similarly positioned in the refractory setting. Now, what would set Cemsidomide apart from those two is its differentiated safety and the upcoming potential for combination therapy. However, it is still very early days, and investors who are buying in early now are hoping it could capture a meaningful share of the market.

Regulatory risks

I would put the main concerns for cemsidomide stock to lie in the upcoming trials. The Phase 2 MOMENTUM trial and Phase 1b combination with elranatamab are typical of late-stage multiple myeloma studies. Safety going forward from here will be critical, especially in heavily pretreated patients. Later down the line, the company needs to post a good package for the FDA, and they'll closely evaluate things like rates of cytopenias, infections, and other immune-related adverse events, as well as treatment discontinuations. I'm expecting them to also assess the durability of response and whether cemsidomide actually adds meaningful benefit in combination regimens without compromising the overall tolerability. For multiple myeloma approvals, the FDA typically looks for a clinically meaningful overall response rate and preferably some complete responses. They would also need to see manageable toxicity and a clear risk-benefit in patients with only a few remaining therapy options.

Valuation

C4's valuation, as you would expect, reflects its pre-revenue status. They're still a clinical-stage biotech with little in sales, so most traditional earnings-based multiples may not be meaningful. Instead, I choose to focus on balance sheet strength and some revenue-based metrics. On a trailing-twelve-month basis, the company trades at an EV to sales ratio of 3.26x, about a 16% discount to the sector median. It tells me the market is valuing C4 a bit more conservatively than peers despite the advancement of late-stage assets. Its price-to-book ratio of 1.17x also screens as cheap, again significantly below the sector median of 2.86x.

So it would appear that shares are trading near asset value and have a limited premium for pipeline optionality. The company holds a market cap of roughly $236 million, cash of $192 million, and an enterprise value of only $98 million. So the valuation effectively ascribes quite a modest value to future cemsidomide commercialisation potential. On the 6-month chart, the stock is showing us some quite good momentum, up about 76%. I'm expecting this momentum to continue through into the readouts in 1Q/2Q26. Right now, the stock trades with an average daily volume of around 1 million shares, which gives some liquidity. Investors should expect to generally enter and exit their positions without major price disruption; however, larger trades could impact the stock, since it holds a relatively small market cap of about $236 million.

Looking ahead

Going forward, investors should keep an eye on the upcoming Phase 2 MOMENTUM trial initiation. We're set to get the topline date in 1Q26, and I'm tipping it to be the next big catalyst for the stock. The Phase 1b combination trial with elranatamab results are set to come after 2Q26, and some kind of FDA alignment on the recommended Phase 2 dose by year-end 2025. Early efficacy and safety readouts from these studies will be key for the company. With shares now trading in the mid-$2 range, I'm quite bullish on C4 and see significant upside ahead.

data.title: Allspring Government Securities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Strategy

  • Seeks to outperform the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Ex-Credit Index by investing in high-quality fixed-income instruments while maintaining a target duration of +/- one year of the benchmark
  • Typically holds most of its exposure in mortgage-backed securities, including collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs), and asset-backed securities issued or guaranteed by U.S. government agencies or government-sponsored enterprises
  • Seeks to invest predominantly in securities rated AAA by Standard & Poor's or an equivalent quality rating from another Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization

Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate ex Credit Bond Index, for the three-month period that ended September 30, 2025.
  • Duration and curve positioning were the largest contributors to performance, while sector and issue selection detracted. Quality had a neutral impact on performance for the quarter.

Market review

The U.S. economy sustained its momentum from the second quarter into the third quarter of 2025, with current activity indicators pointing to a second straight quarter of above-trend growth. Although second-quarter gross domestic product was revised upward due to stronger consumer spending, the first half of the year still posted below-trend growth of 1.6% annualized, weighed down by rapid and chaotic policy shifts. Growth in final sales to private domestic purchasers remained robust, supported by solid consumer and corporate fundamentals. Overall, economic strength continued to be driven by high-income households and large-cap corporations that remained less sensitive to interest rate changes than prior cycles.

Inflation edged higher in the third quarter, leaving year-over-year price growth modestly elevated around 3.0%. Goods inflation was driven by tariff-related price pass-through, while services excluding shelter inflation remained persistently high. Importantly, this worsening inflation trend occurred alongside a cooling labor market. Job growth slowed significantly, with nonfarm payrolls averaging roughly 29,000 over the past three months. The unemployment rate rose slightly to 4.3%, leaving the labor market tight. This was largely due to declining labor supply due to easing net immigration and an aging workforce.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) lowered the policy rate by 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) to a range of 4.00–4.25% in the third quarter, citing a growing balance of risks between its two mandates. Central banks in other developed markets continued their easing cycles, with most approaching their respective neutral rate levels. Geopolitical tensions remained elevated.

The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning

Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—contributor

  • The portfolio maintained a modest long duration positioning relative to the benchmark for most of the period as a hedge against macroeconomic and policy uncertainty, which contributed to performance. We moved to a flat duration posture in mid-September given we see near-term upside risks to yields. We maintained a curve-steepening bias throughout the second quarter expressed as an overweight in 2- and 5-year maturities and an underweight to the longer end. The portfolio benefited as rates fell across the curve during the quarter.
  • The Fed resumed rate cuts in the period amid growing internal divisions over the prioritization of its dual mandate—price stability versus full employment—raising questions about the appropriate pace of further easing. Fed funds ended the period in a range between 4.00% and 4.25%. The U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened during the period.
  • We expect credit conditions to remain healthy. The current outlook suggests one or two more rate cuts in 2025. We are carefully watching macroeconomics data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves.

Quality—neutral

  • Given the fund’s high-quality mandate, credit-quality allocation rarely will be a contributor or detractor versus the benchmark.

Sector—detractor

  • An overweight to securitized sectors detracted from performance during the quarter. The fund’s 1.5% allocation to non-agency collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) was the primary detractor as spreads on deeper discount bonds widened over the period. A 9% overweight to agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) contributed to performance as spreads tightened 5–10 bps.
  • The fund’s overall securitized exposure was close to unchanged over the quarter, with a 0.3% reduction in non-agency CMOs offset by an increase to agency MBS. The fund ended the third quarter with overweights to agency MBS and commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) of 9% and 13%, respectively. The fund’s exposure to out-of-benchmark CMOs was approximately 7%.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Issue selection detracted from performance overall. An overweight to GNMAs detracted, partially offset by an underweight to 15-years and selection within specified pools, which contributed. Positioning within the government sector, CMBS, and asset-backed securities were approximately neutral to performance.
  • Within MBS, we increased the fund’s overweight to higher coupons and reduced an overweight to discount coupons as premium coupons underperformed over the period.

BENCHMARK DESCRIPTIONS:

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate ex Credit Index is composed of the Bloomberg U.S. Government Bond Index and the Bloomberg U.S. Mortgage-Backed Securities Index and includes Treasury issues, agency issues, and mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Index is an unmanaged index of prices of U.S. Treasury bonds with maturities of 1 to 30 years. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Mortgage Backed Securities (MBS) Index tracks fixed-rate agency mortgage backed pass-through securities guaranteed by Ginnie Mae (GNMA), Fannie Mae (FNMA), and Freddie Mac (FHLMC). The index is constructed by grouping individual to-be-announced-deliverable MBS pools into aggregates or generics based on program, coupon, and vintage. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund’s actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody’s rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody’s rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMIG-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond’s price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund’s duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond’s (or fund’s) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: All investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Securities issued by U.S. government agencies or government-sponsored entities may not be guaranteed by the U.S. Treasury. Consult the fund’s prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled intermediate government bond funds, the Government Securities Fund received 2 stars among 212 funds, 3 stars among 203 funds, and 3 stars among 169 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. data.title: Blackstone: I Still Can't Stomach The Valuation
data.description:
Near the end of August, I upgraded Blackstone Inc. (NYSE:BX) from a sell to a hold rating. While the fundamentals showed that the firm was indeed best of breed, the valuation was a bit rich and so I decided to stay neutral on the stock. As you can see in the rating history chart below, Blackstone has experienced significant losses in recent months and today we'll examine their most recent results to see if this is a buying opportunity.

In the analysis below, it is shown that Blackstone's fundamentals remain very solid. Inflows demonstrate client confidence, strong investment performance indicate a good strategy, and robust bottom line results show that profitability is also solid. Like in my previous article, I am impressed by the firm. However, also like in my previous article, the valuation gap between Blackstone and competitors seems too large to be justified. As a result, at this time I have decided to maintain my hold rating despite the firm being very well run.

Inflows Show Healthy Client Activity

Before discussing the financial performance of the firm, let's take a look at their AUM and net flows to see how client activity is holding up. Investment performance can vary quarter to quarter but whether or not Blackstone is attracting ever more capital is a good indication of how clients perceive the firm. As shown above, total AUM hit $1.2417 trillion, up 12% with $54.2 billion of that increase coming from inflows. In the previous quarter, inflows were reported to be $52.1 billion while in the prior year period, it was just $40.5 billion. Therefore, as a whole, it seems clients are increasingly holding a favorable view on Blackstone's capabilities.

For their fee-earning AUM, they reported a 10% increase YoY to $906.2 billion but inflows of $39 billion were slightly less than the $41.9 billion we saw in Q2. Do note that in 2024 Q3, there were inflows of just $34.1 billion and so I would say that investors still don't have too much to complain about. The trustworthiness of the firm in the eyes of clients is shown to be rock solid through these inflow statistics.

Perpetual capital is really a key metric for Blackstone as the ability to invest in assets for the long run is core factor in unlocking value. As you can see above, they have hit $500.6 billion in total perpetual capital AUM and that represents a growth rate of 15%. In Q2, growth was reported to be 16% but still this Q3 increase outpaces the firm's total AUM growth rate. Therefore, I believe this indicates that Blackstone's long term focus is resonating with clients and in the long term, that should drive both client and shareholder value.

Investment Performance Is Solid

As stated above, investment performance can fluctuate and may not be the greatest indication of the long term success of a firm like Blackstone that's in for the long haul. However, Q3 figures show that the firm's investment strategy is likely working out. Apart from Real Estate, Blackstone's investment performance was very respectable and so you can see that there was broad strength. For real estate, they experienced depreciation in opportunistic both for Q3 and the LTM. Therefore, it seems commercial real estate remains weak relative to other asset classes. Again, they are in for the long haul and so the firm likely sees a recovery down the road.

Ready To Pounce

Blackstone's dry powder is up significantly from the previous quarter's $181.2 billion and the prior year period's $171.6 billion and so it seems that the firm is well-positioned to pounce on opportunities ahead. When comparing the pie chart on the right to the one for Q2, there are some notable differences. Firstly, dry powder for multi-asset investing and for private equity declined very slightly. Dry powder for private equity is actually very strong as they deployed $5.564 billion in Q3. For real estate, there was a $2.5 billion QoQ drop in dry powder after a $3.6 billion deployment in the quarter. Therefore, overall, it seems that clients aren't overly encouraged by what they see in that asset class. That's isn't too surprising given the performance figures shown earlier.

The most impressive dry powder metric is the $9.6 billion QoQ increase for credit & insurance. In Q3 alone, Blackstone deployed $16.656 billion in this asset class and so the considerable increase in dry powder indicates great interest. At this point, it seems that private credit is booming. Overall, the strength in dry powder indicates that the firm is ready to pounce but it is clear that investors are steering clear of real estate in relative terms.

Business Is Good

Finally, we arrive at the bottom line and results indicate a very profitable business. In fee-related terms, there was a 26% increase YoY in earnings as performance revenues soared 72%. Clearly, the successful execution of their investment strategy is powering growth for the firm.

For net realizations, there was an increase of 124%. As you can see above, while the realized performance compensation increase of 92% may seem a bit concerning to investors, note that realized performance revenues increased by an even greater margin of 117%. Therefore, it seems that profitability is solid.

At the end of the day, DE per common share was reported to be $1.52, up 50% YoY which is quite an acceleration from Q2's 26% increase. This figure also beat expectations by a rather impressive margin of $0.29 and so Q3 was overall a robust quarter for Blackstone in terms of their earnings.

Valuation Still Expensive

In my previous article, I compared Blackstone's P/B ratio to competitors KKR (KKR) and Apollo (APO) and concluded that the valuation gap was likely excessive. As you can see above, while the P/B ratio has contracted for all three companies, the valuation deviation between Blackstone and competitors has closed somewhat since late August. Blackstone is undoubtedly the leader in alternative assets but I still believe the valuation is somewhat rich.

Currently, Seeking Alpha rates Blackstone's growth as a B+ and their profitability as an A- relative to the Financials sector. While that is impressive note that KKR is rated as a B for growth and an A- for profitability. Therefore, while Blackstone's fundamentals still have the edge, do they really justify a P/B premium of around ~237%? I don't believe so and that's why I view the valuation as still a bit too expensive for an entry right now. As of this writing, Seeking Alpha rates Blackstone's valuation as an F overall, confirming my evaluation.

Technical Analysis

In terms of the technicals, there are some mixed signals but overall, there are some good signs for the bulls. As you can see, the 50 day SMA is falling towards the 200 day SMA and so the dreaded death cross may happen in the not so distant future. However, there are some bottoming signs.

Firstly, the 9 day EMA is closing the gap with the 21 day EMA as a signal that near term bearish momentum is receding. Secondly, the stock has reclaimed the Bollinger Bands midline on the current trading day as another indication that the bears may now be in retreat. The MACD and RSI also indicate that there may be bullish reversal potential as both showed positive divergence at the recent November low. You can see that both indicators remained above their lows of earlier this month. Furthermore, while the RSI has yet to regain 50, the MACD recently had a significant bullish crossover. Overall, while a death cross may be unavoidable, there are many other signals that indicate better days are ahead for the stock.

Takeaway

It's not a bad idea to buy a best of breed business at a fair valuation but is the premium over KKR really fair? Blackstone has the stronger fundamentals (growth & profitability) overall according to Seeking Alpha and is the leader in this space but the nearly 240% P/B ratio premium is simply too much to stomach for me personally. The stock is seeing bullish reversal potential as shown above and this elevated valuation relative to peers may continue as investors perceive the firm to be the best in the business. To be more conservative however, I have decided to maintain my hold rating on Blackstone despite being thoroughly impressed by their fundamental robustness.

data.title: American Century Short Duration Strategic Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Bonds advanced for the quarter. U.S. investment-grade bonds logged their third straight quarterly gain. Labor market weakness and growing expectations for the Federal Reserve (Fed) to cut interest rates largely supported the bond market’s positive performance.

The economy bounced back. After contracting in the first quarter, the U.S. economy rallied back in the second quarter with a 3.8% annualized gain. Data released in the third quarter showed the manufacturing and services sectors expanded, and retail sales were robust, but the labor market weakened. The government overstated job growth for the 12 months ended in March by nearly 1 million jobs.

The Fed cut rates. The Fed cut rates by 0.25% in September, marking the first easing move since December. The target lending rate range dropped to 4% to 4.25%. Amid signs of a weakening job market, policymakers indicated rising unemployment represents a bigger economic threat than stubborn inflation. Most officials penciled in another 0.5% of easing by year-end.

Treasury yields declined modestly. Amid resilient growth and persistent inflation, Treasury yields were volatile but ended the quarter lower overall. Labor market woes and growing expectations for Fed easing generally drove the decline. The two-year Treasury yield declined 0.1% to 3.62%, while the 10-year yield fell 0.08% to 4.15%. The yield curve between two and 10 years steepened slightly.

Inflation data were mixed. The annual headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose from 2.7% in July to 2.9% in August, its highest since January. Core CPI remained unchanged at 3.1%, and the annual core personal consumption expenditures price index, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, held steady at 2.9%, well above the Fed’s 2% target. Inflation break-even rates rose modestly, and Treasury inflation-protected securities outperformed nominal Treasuries.

All sectors posted gains. Treasury yields declined, credit spreads tightened, and all investment-grade bond sectors advanced in the quarter, led by the outperforming corporate bond and mortgage-backed securities sectors. Treasuries advanced but lagged bonds. High-yield corporates slightly lagged investment-grade corporates.

Portfolio Performance Review

Sector allocations drove results. An out-of-index position in high-yield credit was a main driver of the portfolio’s outperformance compared with the benchmark. Positions in banking and media and entertainment were top contributors. An overweight relative to the benchmark to the securitized sector also contributed. Our preference for out-of-index credit-sensitive securitized securities lifted performance.

Security selection aided performance. Security selection among investment-grade corporates was also a key driver of performance compared with the benchmark. Our selections in the financials sector, primarily banks, offered notable results. We lifted exposure to the sector, favoring higher-quality, higher-yielding securities. Meanwhile, our selections among emerging markets bonds slightly detracted.

Yield curve strategy contributed. Yield curve exposure modestly contributed to results compared with the benchmark, largely due to duration positioning. Pricing dynamics in the short-term Treasury market prompted us to reduce duration, but the portfolio’s duration remained longer than the index’s. Carry effects also boosted performance.

Portfolio Positioning

The economy may stall. We believe below-trend growth(annualized gross domestic product of less than 1.5%) may unfold, as the cumulative effects of restrictive monetary policy and tariff uncertainties weigh on the economy. Lower labor supply and waning demand should generate a labor market in which hiring and firing remain relatively low. Growth should improve next year amid fiscal and monetary support.

Credit metrics remain solid. In our view, the corporate credit backdrop remains upbeat. Fundamentals are sound, we feel yields are attractive, and defaults are low. This should fuel robust mergers and acquisitions activity, which could have mixed implications for the market. Additionally, we will closely monitor the effects of tariffs on corporate profitability and margins, consumer spending and risk appetites.

Searching for themes. With valuations near historically tight levels, we continue to focus on themes to uncover value. Specifically, we look for bonds offering spread tightening potential due to the issuers’ exposure to positive events or catalysts. Our stringent credit research helps uncover these securities. As always, we will remain nimble, highly selective and focused on valuations.
